reasoning: Peer family is a dense hard-edged dungeon tileset (stone walls, floors, dungeon objects) rendered in classic pixel art with crisp per-pixel detail, defined dark outlines, directional lighting, and a palette of dark grays, blacks, teal-cyan, crimson-red, and gold. (2) OUTLINE COLOR: peers use near-black; candidate has none — fail. (3) SHADOW DIRECTION: peers show consistent top-left directional shading; candidate is radially symmetric with no directional shadow — fail. (4) SHADOW COUNT: peers use single-pass shadow; candidate zero — fail. (5) HIGHLIGHT INTENSITY: peers have modest 1-2px bright specular dots; candidate blows out to a large saturated white diamond core — overdriven, fail. (6) COLOR TEMPERATURE: peers are cool-neutral stone with selective warm accents; candidate is warm-pink/rose center bleeding into cool blue rim — palette register mismatch, marginal. (7) DETAIL DENSITY: peers pack intricate crack/texture/object detail into every tile at this scale; candidate is smooth concentric gradient rings with minimal internal detail — severe fail. (8) PROPORTION: circular radial form vs rectangular tile cells — acceptable for item-type difference. (9) RENDERING STYLE: hard-edged pixel art (peers) vs soft blended gradient glow (candidate) — fundamental pipeline mismatch, fail. (10) PALETTE OVERLAP: teal-cyan rim shows some overlap with peer teal tile borders, but pink/rose tones are entirely absent from the peer sheet — partial fail. (11) CHARM LEVEL: peer set is grim/utilitarian dungeon architecture with zero kawaii affect; candidate reads as soft pastel magical portal, more akin to a healing or fantasy-RPG aesthetic, mismatched register — fail. The item-type exemption covers shape differences but not the wholesale rendering-language gap. Six of eleven dimensions are hard fails.
